post: The generation in classrooms today will carry the torch of self-government forward. Whether that happens depends largely on what teachers have to work with. Civics Champion has helped fulfill 6,500+ classroom requests – and put 50,000 Pocket Constitutions in students' hands – since last spring. There's a teacher near you who could use your support right now. 👉  https://bit.ly/4coYN0s  #CivicsChampion  #CivicEducation  #TeacherSupport  #Constitution

The people shaping civics in the Seattle region will be in the same room on April 30. We've brought together educators, civic leaders, and technology partners for a forum at MOHAI built around real conversation and collaborative problem-solving. If you work at the intersection of education, technology, or community then this is your room. Six days out. A few seats remain. April 30 | 6:30-9:30 PM | Museum of History and Industry, Seattle Register here: 🔗  https://bit.ly/3OOH0He  #CivicEducation  #Civics  #EdTech  #Seattle  #MOHAI  #CivicsInnovation  #BillOfRightsInstitute

Thomas Jefferson believed in natural rights, civic virtue, and self-government more eloquently than almost anyone in American history. He also knew slavery was wrong, and spent his life rationalizing why he couldn't act on that knowledge. What do we do with a founder who articulated the ideals of a free society but failed to live up to them? Historian Andrew Burstein argues we start by understanding before we judge. In this episode of BRI Scholar Talks, Burstein joins Tony Williams to examine the character behind the Declaration: the intellectual brilliance, the moral contradictions, and the enduring questions Jefferson's legacy leaves for every generation. Watch it here:  https://bit.ly/4tpRYBU  #AmericanHistory  #CivicEducation  #TeachingHistory  #SocialStudies  #BRI

Know a history or civics teacher doing exceptional work this year? Tell them about the Civic Star Challenge. Every teacher who shares a Declaration or A250 classroom moment is entered in the weekly $300 drawing, and there's no reason any of them should miss the final cycle. The more the merrier. Share this post. Forward the link. Let's make sure no great classroom story goes unrecognized. Final cycle ends May 15:  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #TeacherCommunity  #America250  #CivicsTeacher  #ShareWithAColleague

13 days ago, 4 astronauts returned from the Moon. The question they left behind is one your students should be working through right now. The Artemis II mission made history on April 10, the first crewed lunar flyby in more than half a century. The mission was made possible by NASA's $24.4 billion federal budget and decades of sustained public investment in space exploration. Whether that investment should grow is a genuinely open question, and a civics-rich one. Think the Vote's latest debate: Should the U. S. government increase funding for space exploration and research? On one side: the argument that space investment drives technological innovation, strengthens national security in a competitive global environment, and inspires the next generation of STEM talent. GPS, satellite communications, and advances in medical imaging all have roots in space research. This is the case for exploration as forward-looking public investment. On the other: the argument that federal resources are finite, that domestic needs from infrastructure to healthcare remain pressing, and that private companies are increasingly capable of advancing space exploration without expanded government spending. This is a debate about national priorities, the proper scope of government, and how a republic decides where limited public resources go. Those are questions citizens, including the students in your classroom, have both the right and the responsibility to engage. Student submissions close May 7. Share it this week. 👉  https://bit.ly/4tu0UGE  #CivicsEducation  #ThinktheVote  #Artemis  #SpaceExploration  #NASA  #SocialStudies  #HighSchoolTeachers  #CriticalThinking  #BillofRightsInstitute  #STEMEducation

The MyImpact Challenge deadline is May 3. That's just 12 days. For teachers who've been meaning to submit with their students, or for students who've been working on a project but haven't started the application, here's exactly what a complete submission looks like. 3 components are required: 1. A written essay connecting the project to America's founding principles and civic virtues: what motivated the students, what values guided their work, and what they learned. 2. A project report documenting what was done, how, and what impact it had on the community. 3. Photo or video documentation: a visual record of the project in action. That's it. There's no trifold. No in-person presentation. No travel required. It's submitted entirely through the online portal at the link below, and it's open to students anywhere in the country: all 50 states, U. S. territories, and Armed Forces Schools Abroad. If you're working with students who have a real project underway, the essay is often the only piece that isn't already done. And with 12 days left, there's still time to finish it. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #ServiceLearning  #BRI

BRI was proud to sponsor the 3rd annual Democracy Bowl at the University of Pittsburgh-Johnstown earlier this month. Middle and high school students competed in history and civics-themed trivia and showcased the civics projects they've built in their communities. This is the generation that carries the torch forward. Days like this make it clear they're ready. Thank you to our partner Founding Forward for being part of the event. See the highlights 👇  https://bit.ly/4mHM50k  #DemocracyBowl  #UPJ  #Civics

There's a narrative out there that civic engagement is declining. That students don't care. That history class is a checkbox. Teachers in the Civic Star Challenge are proving otherwise, 1 classroom moment at a time. If civics is alive in your room this year, share it. Enter the weekly $300 drawing. And let your classroom be part of a bigger story. This cycle closes May 15:  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #CivicsIsAlive  #America250  #CivicsTeacher  #BRI

What does it look like when educators, civic leaders, and tech partners are actually in the same room? We're about to find out. On April 30, the Civics Innovation Lab at BRI is hosting a civic forum at the Museum of History and Industry in Seattle, built around cross-sector conversation, collaborative problem-solving, and the question of what civic education needs to look like for the next generation. It's ten days away. Seats are going fast. April 30 | 6:30-9:30 PM | MOHAI, Seattle Register here: 🔗  https://bit.ly/3OOH0He  #Civics  #CivicsEducation  #MOHAI  #Seattle
